item.title: PNG foregoes billions to attract investment: IRC
item.description: THE Internal Revenue Commission (IRC) says that tax incentives offered to businesses in the country are intended to attract investments, grow the economy and develop remote areas. The IRC was responding to the World Bank Group’s PNG Public Finance Policy Review 2026 which says that the country is losing billions of Kina in revenue because of tax exemptions and incentives.
item.content:encoded:
THE Internal Revenue Commission (IRC) says that tax incentives offered to businesses in the country are intended to attract investments, grow the economy and develop remote areas.

The IRC was responding to the World Bank Group’s PNG Public Finance Policy Review 2026 which says that the country is losing billions of Kina in revenue because of tax exemptions and incentives.

The IRC’s tax intelligence division explained in a statement that the country foregoes this revenue through tax expenditure – a term used to describe money not collected by the government because of tax breaks it gives out.

These breaks include tax exemptions, deductions, credits, and lower tax rates given to companies in certain sectors or locations.

The IRC said that in 2024 alone, Papua New Guinea did not collect K2.465 billion in goods and services tax (GST) revenue.

Of that, K1.926 billion came from zero-rated sales and K495.6 million from exempt sales.

In 2021, the revenue not collected from GST was K2.201 billion.

It climbed to K2.440 billion in 2022 and K2.485 billion in 2023 before a slight drop in 2024.

Import duty exemptions have also grown sharply.

PNG did not collect K27.4 million in 2021 in import duties, but by 2024 that number had jumped to K121.1 million, a more than fourfold increase.

The IRC says most of the 2024 increase came from exemptions in the non-mining sector.

The companies are expected to create jobs, train local workers, build public infrastructure such as roads, schools and hospitals, and eventually pay taxes once their holiday periods end.

 A 10-year tax holiday is offered to new businesses set up in prescribed areas.

Mining and petroleum projects could also receive long-term protections tied to the life of their projects.

The commission also noted other types of incentives the partial exemptions, tax credits for infrastructure spending, accelerated depreciation of assets, and customs duty relief on imported machinery and materials.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Public warned against leaving fires unattended
item.description: THE general public have been warned to avoid starting grass or bush fires and not to leave cooking fire, candles or burning rubbish unattended because the fire could spread quickly. National Capital District Fire Service Commander Chief Supt Sedrick Luwai said the dry conditions could increase the risk of bushfires, structural fires, and rapid fire spread.
item.content:encoded:
THE general public have been warned to avoid starting grass or bush fires and not to leave cooking fire, candles or burning rubbish unattended because the fire could spread quickly.

National Capital District Fire Service Commander Chief Supt Sedrick Luwai said the dry conditions could increase the risk of bushfires, structural fires, and rapid fire spread.

“Supervise children and educate them not to play with fire, dispose of cigarette butts safely and immediately report fire emergencies to the nearest fire station,” he said.

Luwai said the advice from the National Weather Service and the National Disaster Centre was that dry conditions were expected to affect the NCD, Gulf, Western and the Highlands provinces.

Fireman at the four fire stations in the NCD have started fire safety awareness in the city in preparation for the dry season.

“This is to educate the public on the danger of careless fire use, including setting fire to grass and bushes,” he said.

“In May, the four stations have responded to 31 grass fire incidents in NCD, highlighting the increased fire risk during dry conditions.

Meanwhile, Farmers and Settlers Association president Wilson Thompson warned farmers to prepare for the dry season.

“Some parts of the country are still experiencing rain and this is the chance for farmers to prepare in advance,” Thompson said.

“It’s raining in Morobe, so that is a good time to cut, do pruning when there’s rain and shelter because when the dry season hits farmers will have to concentrate on other things.”

Thompson advised farmers to start conserving water sources, using drought resistant plants and planting crops like yam, taro, cassava, ferns or breadfruit.

“If the source is from a stream they need to make sure they don’t cut the trees or the bushes for shade,” he said.

“And if you have tanks, clean them out to store water.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Five groups receive K50,000 each from Malaysian association
item.description: FIVE non-profit and healthcare charity organisations in Port Moresby have received financial boosts from the Malaysian Association of Papua New Guinea (MAPNG) to support their community and life-saving work.  The association presented K50,000 each to the organisations in the second round of its annual donation drive yesterday in Port Moresby.
item.content:encoded:
FIVE non-profit and healthcare charity organisations in Port Moresby have received financial boosts from the Malaysian Association of Papua New Guinea (MAPNG) to support their community and life-saving work. 

The association presented K50,000 each to the organisations in the second round of its annual donation drive yesterday in Port Moresby.

The recipients include City Mission PNG Ltd, the PNG Kidney Foundation, Operation Open Heart, the Sir Buri Kidu Heart Institute Inc, and the PNG Children’s Foundation.  

The charity organisations dedicate their resources to improving society through a variety of essential healthcare, welfare, and education initiatives.

MAPNG president William Ling said that the association deeply appreciated the contribution these groups made in the country.

“We appreciate the work and contribution of these organisations and will continue to support them in the coming years,” Ling said. 

The funds distributed for this year’s donations were raised during the annual charity golf fundraiser hosted by MAPNG.

 To remain eligible, MAPNG requires all recipients to maintain transparency and comply with their standards. 

Two of the recipients, Operation Open Heart and the Sir Buri Kidu Heart Institute, share a joint mission to deliver critical cardiac care across the country. 

Jane Huka, director of corporate affairs for Operation Open Heart and executive director of Sir Buri Kidu Heart Institute, said that the funding was crucial for maintaining their daily medical operations. 

“The donation that we get from MAPNG goes into helping our cardiac clinicians, both doctors and nurses,” Huka said.

“Every year they go for conferences, workshops and training outside of the country for development and up-skilling.”  

Huka further noted that the funds given to the Sir Buri Kidu Heart Institute would directly support the diagnostic clinic located at the Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH). 

The institute works closely with the PMGH cardiac clinic to screen patients.

Once patients requiring heart surgery or specialised treatment are identified, Operation Open Heart steps in to bring in specialists.

The donated funds will also be used to buy medical equipment, consumables, and cover any financial shortfalls that the hospital’s clinic cannot accommodate. 

The PNG Children’s Foundation said these funds would directly impact vulnerable youth.

The foundation extended its gratitude to MAPNG for the generous contributions.

Yiannis Nicolaou, president of the PNG Children’s Foundation, noted that this act of kindness and generosity would make a meaningful difference in the lives of these children, adding that he hoped this would inspire more sponsors to come forward to help create a brighter future for them.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Parties urged to collaborate
item.description: POLITICAL parties must move away from being focused on having an individualistic way of operating to becoming institutions capable of enduring challenges on their own, an official says. Political Parties registrar Emmanuel Pok said: “For too long, political parties in PNG have revolved around individuals, surfacing during elections or confidence votes, and then retreating into obscurity.
item.content:encoded:
POLITICAL parties must move away from being focused on having an individualistic way of operating to becoming institutions capable of enduring challenges on their own, an official says.

Political Parties registrar Emmanuel Pok said: “For too long, political parties in PNG have revolved around individuals, surfacing during elections or confidence votes, and then retreating into obscurity.

“Through the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission and Office of the Registrar, we now have mechanisms to strengthen political parties as enduring institutions and not just electoral machines,” he said.

Pok made these remarks in the magazine titled, “A Chronicle of Political Parties in Papua New Guinea” that was launched by the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission in Port Moresby on May 8.

He said that the passage of the Organic Law on the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates (OLIPPAC) in 2001 marked a turning point in Papua New Guinea’s political history as it gave political parties legal standing, funding, and structure.

“It divided our politics into two eras – pre-OLIPPAC and post-OLIPPAC,” he said.

“Following the 2002 elections, Sir Michael Somare’s National Alliance, though holding just 15 seats, was invited to form government under OLIPPAC provisions.

“For the first time since independence, a government lasted a full five-year term.

“Political stability brought economic confidence, with growth averaging between 8-13 percent and record surpluses driving new development.

“In 2010 the Supreme Court struck down key sections of the law, reopening the door to party hopping and restoring the volatility of motions of no confidence.

“The political impasse of 2011 that saw Peter O’Neill unseat Somare highlighted both the strengths and weaknesses of the law.

“Ironically, today’s Prime Minister, James Marape, could not have risen in 2019 had those nullified provisions remained in force,” he said.

Meanwhile, Pok told a conference in Port Moresby attended by representatives from the 44 political parties currently registered of the new provisions in the revised OLIPPAC that was passed with an 83-0 vote on March 24, which include:

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Minj hosts DEM Cup double
item.description: ROUND 7 of the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup kicks off tomorrow at the Jonah Ambane Oval in Minj, Jiwaka, with a double-header featuring the Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles against the Petroleum Resources Kutubu (PRK) Gulf Isou. Both sides have struggled for consistency this season. The Eagles sit eighth with four points, while Isou remain second from bottom with only two. The reigning 2024 premiers, Eagles, wi
item.content:encoded:
ROUND 7 of the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup kicks off tomorrow at the Jonah Ambane Oval in Minj, Jiwaka, with a double-header featuring the Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles against the Petroleum Resources Kutubu (PRK) Gulf Isou.

Both sides have struggled for consistency this season. The Eagles sit eighth with four points, while Isou remain second from bottom with only two. The reigning 2024 premiers, Eagles, will be chasing back-to-back wins after upsetting last year’s champions Lae Snax Tigers 24-10 last weekend — their second victory of the year.

In the second match, third-placed Kandes face hosts Asila Waghi Tumbe.

With nine points, Kandes could move level with the competition leaders if they secure a win.

Tumbe, sitting 10th with four points, will be desperate to defend their home turf after last week’s loss to Goroka Lahanis.

In Rabaul, the ENB Agmark Gurias host the New Porgera Ltd Enga Mioks at Queen Elizabeth Park. The Mioks, ninth on the ladder, have shown improvement but will need discipline and strong defence to upset the Gurias.

Sunday’s double-header at the Sir John Guise Stadium in Port Moresby sees the Gas Resources Central Dabaris take on the struggling Tigers, who sit bottom with just two points. Another defeat could endanger their finals hopes.

The second clash pits Moni Plus Port Moresby Vipers against the Lahanis.

Round 7 concludes in Lae, where leaders Muruks face fourth-placed Kroton Hela Wigmen.

Muruks and Vipers are currently tied at the top with 10 points each, both showing consistency as the season heats up.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Oceania athletes tally mixed results
item.description: DAY three of the Oceania Athletics Championships came to a close on Wednesday evening in Darwin, Australia, with Papua New Guinea’s athletes producing a mix of seasoned performances and exciting breakthroughs. In the men’s 100 metres final, Pais Wisil finished fourth as Australia claimed a clean sweep of the medals.
item.content:encoded:
DAY three of the Oceania Athletics Championships came to a close on Wednesday evening in Darwin, Australia, with Papua New Guinea’s athletes producing a mix of seasoned performances and exciting breakthroughs.

In the men’s 100 metres final, Pais Wisil finished fourth as Australia claimed a clean sweep of the medals.

Earlier in the semi-finals, Timothy Tuna ran 10.64 seconds and Leroy Kamau 10.83 seconds, but both narrowly missed out on a place in the final.

There was strong progress in the hurdles, with Daniel Baul qualifying comfortably for the men’s 400m hurdles final after clocking an impressive 47.82 seconds on Tuesday evening.

Meanwhile, 18-year-old Hephzibah Romalus from Lae announced herself as a rising sprint talent, recording 12.42 seconds in the women’s 100m heats.

She will now turn her focus to the Under-20 event.

In the field events, veteran thrower Sharon Toako secured victory in the master’s javelin with a throw of 43.17m.

Papua New Guinea will also be represented by three athletes in the men’s javelin competition, adding to the nation’s strong presence in the throws.

Looking ahead, PNG’s campaign continues with plenty of action still to come.

The men’s 200m, 800m, 110m hurdles and 4x100m relay are on the schedule, while in the U20 category Jack Mahuru, Ethan Asimba and Romalus will carry the flag for the next generation of Papua New Guinea athletics.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Hunters to regroup, eye changes
item.description: THE SP PNG Hunters will use this weekend’s bye round to regroup and look at possible changes to their line-up ahead of their round 12 clash against the Tweed Seagulls next month. The Hunters return to action on Saturday, June 6, at the Preston Building Sports Complex after suffering another disappointing defeat to the South Logan Magpies, a 20-30 loss, in round 10 last weekend in Port Moresby, con
item.content:encoded:
THE SP PNG Hunters will use this weekend’s bye round to regroup and look at possible changes to their line-up ahead of their round 12 clash against the Tweed Seagulls next month.

The Hunters return to action on Saturday, June 6, at the Preston Building Sports Complex after suffering another disappointing defeat to the South Logan Magpies, a 20-30 loss, in round 10 last weekend in Port Moresby, continuing a difficult season for the club.

The latest loss leaves the Hunters with only two wins from their opening 10 matches and sitting second last on the competition ladder. The bye round now comes at an important time for the Paul Aiton coached-side as they look for ways to improve consistency and possibly reshuffle the team combination for the remaining matches.

Adding to the challenge is a growing injury crisis, with the club confirming that 11 players are currently receiving treatment, assessment or rehabilitation.

Meanwhile, the high injury toll has seen several players return to contention for spots in the side with hooker Lynchill Kiap playing out 80 minutes for the first time for the Hunters in the game against the Magpies.

Used mainly as bench dummy-half replacement for much of his career, Kiap has been required to play more minutes and has acquitted himself well making 36 tackles, several dummy-half runs which netted 10-metres or more and providing good service to halves Jamie Mavoko and Joel Gena.

Outside back Hercules Kondo also debuted last Sunday in a robust display of hard-running wing play.

The Eastern Highlander took his opportunity after injuries to Elijah Roltinga, Myles Banam and Douglas David required Aiton to blood in another squad member.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Hetura, Mystics clash
item.description: TOMORROW’s opening match in the Port Moresby Netball Association (PomNA) premier division challenge will feature Hetura and Mystics, who will set the stage for the next two premier games to follow. As the first-round battles continue at the Rita Flynn courts, Hetura and Mystics will kick off their match at 1pm, with both sides determined to secure a win after suffering losses in their previous gam
item.content:encoded:
TOMORROW’s opening match in the Port Moresby Netball Association (PomNA) premier division challenge will feature Hetura and Mystics, who will set the stage for the next two premier games to follow.

As the first-round battles continue at the Rita Flynn courts, Hetura and Mystics will kick off their match at 1pm, with both sides determined to secure a win after suffering losses in their previous games last week.

At 2pm, CC Sparrows will take on Haidana Lightning. Sparrows will look to extend their winning streak, while Haidana faces a tough challenge against the defending champions and will need to dig deep into their playbook to keep pace with the giants.

The main game at 3pm will see Telstars clash with Veupunama. Veupunama, cruising through this round of the challenge, will aim to test different combinations, while Telstars, backed by a few experienced players, will be eager to mount a strong challenge.

PomNA president Alurigo Ravusiro said the competition is nearing the end of its first round, with teams becoming more competitive — a positive sign for tough battles, especially in the premier and senior divisions.

Meanwhile, PomNA has reminded all affiliated clubs of important requirements regarding game-day conduct, venue management, communication protocols, and expected behavior. These measures are designed to ensure a safe, respectful, organized, and family-friendly environment for players, officials, supporters, children, and the wider netball community.

Ravusiro thanked all clubs for their cooperation and continued commitment to making PomNA a safe, respectful, and enjoyable competition for everyone.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Human and social toll of illegal mining
item.description: THE issue of illegal miners stealing gold from the Porgera Gold Mine has become a serious concern for Porgera residents, the Enga government, and the Government. These unlawful activities not only cause significant financial losses to the mine and the State, but also threaten safety, peace, and stability in the Porgera Valley.
item.content:encoded:
THE issue of illegal miners stealing gold from the Porgera Gold Mine has become a serious concern for Porgera residents, the Enga government, and the Government.

These unlawful activities not only cause significant financial losses to the mine and the State, but also threaten safety, peace, and stability in the Porgera Valley.

Many illegal miners are criminal elements operating with illegal firearms, engaging in violence, intimidation, and lawlessness, creating fear among residents and mine workers.

Additionally, settlers and migrants from Tari, Laiagam, Kandep, Paiela, and other districts have contributed to social disorder, resource strain, and conflicts with local landowners.

Porgera Gold Mine is a vital economic asset, generating revenue, employment, and development benefits.

The Government must strengthen security by deploying more police or defence personnel and supporting gun surrender programmes to remove illegal firearms.

Community leaders, churches, youth groups, and local authorities should also promote dialogue, peace, and respect for the law.

Unchecked illegal mining and violence could threaten the mine’s operation, negatively affecting Porgera, Enga, and the nation.

Urgent, coordinated action is essential to restore law and order, safeguard communities, and ensure that the benefits of the mine are enjoyed safely and lawfully for current and future generations.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Roads boost access and affordability
item.description: I COMMEND the Government for the Connect PNG programme that is delivering for the people of Papua New Guinea by linking long-dreamed-of roads. The Milne Bay–Port Moresby road is now complete, and soon the Port Moresby–Lae link will be commissioned after final work on the remaining 20km.
item.content:encoded:
I COMMEND the Government for the Connect PNG programme that is delivering for the people of Papua New Guinea by linking long-dreamed-of roads.

The Milne Bay–Port Moresby road is now complete, and soon the Port Moresby–Lae link will be commissioned after final work on the remaining 20km.

This is a huge relief for the Highlands, Madang, and Morobe communities, that have long struggled with expensive air travel.

Similarly, people from Gulf and Central can now explore new landscapes and cultures.

This vital road promotes economic growth and provides an affordable, safe way to travel, connecting communities rather than encouraging regionalism or crime.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Empower local workforce
item.description: FOREIGNERS who cannot communicate fluently in English are occupying key spaces in Papua New Guinea, while our own citizens are sidelined. Many lack the technical skills or teaching ability to contribute to national development.
item.content:encoded:
FOREIGNERS who cannot communicate fluently in English are occupying key spaces in Papua New Guinea, while our own citizens are sidelined.

Many lack the technical skills or teaching ability to contribute to national development.

English and local talent must be prioritised to advance education and growth.

If leaders trust foreign partners, they should personally trade with them rather than allowing outsiders to dominate markets.

PNG nationals are capable of running businesses, supplying markets, and providing services.

Despite promises by Prime Minister James Marape in 2020 to remove foreign dominance, little has changed.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Governance at a standstill
item.description: WHAT if all Papua New Guinean citizens refused to vote in General Election 2027? Under our constitutional democracy, members of Parliament serve fixed five-year terms, granted authority directly by the people.
item.content:encoded:
WHAT if all Papua New Guinean citizens refused to vote in General Election 2027?

Under our constitutional democracy, members of Parliament serve fixed five-year terms, granted authority directly by the people.

Once their term expires, they cannot continue without re-election.

If no votes were cast nationwide, there would be no newly elected Parliament, leaving the country in a constitutional vacuum.

In such a scenario, the governor-general might oversee limited caretaker arrangements to maintain essential State functions, but caretaker authorities cannot pass new laws, make major policy decisions, or exercise full executive power.

The Electoral Commission would be forced to organise fresh elections, and the Supreme Court could be called to interpret governance rules during this unprecedented crisis.

Without an elected Parliament, national budgets could stall, development projects halt, and international agreements face uncertainty.

Public confidence in democracy could erode, and political and economic stability might suffer.

Most importantly, the people’s voice – the foundation of democracy – would vanish.

Voting is not merely about supporting candidates; it is the legal mechanism that empowers citizens, maintains constitutional order, and safeguards the nation’s stability for present and future generations of PNG.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”
